items.fact: DRI includes RDA (Recommended Dietary Allowance) and AI (Adequate Intake) as nutrient intake goals; RDA meets needs of most healthy individuals, while AI is used when evidence is insufficient for RDA.
items.source_context: The Dietary Reference Intakes (DRI) include two sets of nutrient intake goals for individuals—the Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) and Adequate Intake (AI). The RDA reflects the average daily amount of a nutrient considered adequate to meet the needs of most healthy people. If there is insufficient evidence to determine an RDA, an AI is set.

items.fact: EER represents the average dietary energy intake level predicted to maintain energy balance in a healthy individual of specified age, gender, weight, height, and physical activity level.
items.source_context: The Estimated Energy Requirement (EER) represents the average dietary energy intake that will maintain energy balance in a healthy person of a given gender, age, weight, height, and physical activity level.

items.fact: UL is the highest average daily nutrient intake level likely to pose no risk of adverse health effects for most healthy people; absence of a UL does not imply safety at high intakes.
items.source_context: The DRI also include the Tolerable Upper Intake Level (UL) that represents the estimated maximum daily amount of a nutrient that appears safe for most healthy people to consume on a regular basis. Note that the absence of a UL for a nutrient does not indicate that it is safe to consume in high doses, but only that research is too limited to set a UL.

items.fact: Water AI includes water from beverages, drinking water, and food; beverages contribute ~70–80% of total water intake.
items.source_context: aThe water AI includes drinking water, water in beverages, and water in foods; in general, drinking water and other beverages contribute about 70 to 80 percent, and foods, the remainder.

items.fact: ALA is the omega-3 fatty acid referenced as 'linolenic acid' in DRI tables and recommendations.
items.source_context: cThe linolenic acid referred to in this table and text is the omega-3 fatty acid known as alpha-linolenic acid.

items.fact: Protein RDA is expressed both in absolute grams per day and relative to body weight (g/kg/day), with values decreasing slightly with age in adults and increasing during pregnancy and lactation.
items.source_context: Protein RDA (g/day) and Protein RDA (g/kg/day) columns show age- and life-stage–specific values; e.g., increases to 1.10 g/kg/day in 2nd/3rd trimester and 1.30 g/kg/day in early lactation.

items.fact: Total fiber AI increases with age in children and peaks in adolescence/adulthood (e.g., 38 g/day for males 14–18 y), then declines modestly after age 50 (30 g/day).
items.source_context: Total Fiber AI (g/day) column shows values: 19 (1–3 y), 25 (4–8 y), 31 (9–13 y), 38 (14–18 y), 38 (19–30 y), 30 (>50 y) for males.

items.fact: Linoleic acid (omega-6) AI remains stable across most adult life stages (17 g/day for males, 12–13 g/day for females), with slight reductions in older adulthood.
items.source_context: Linoleic Acid AI (g/day): males 17 (14–18 y through >50 y); females 10–13 g/day across reproductive and postmenopausal ages.

items.fact: Adequate Intake (AI) for sodium is 1500 mg/day for most children aged 9–13 years and adults aged 19–50 years, decreasing to 1300 mg/day for adults 51–70 years and 1200 mg/day for those over 70.
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Minerals — Sodium AI (mg/day) table

items.fact: AI for potassium is 4700 mg/day for most individuals aged 14 years and older, including pregnant and lactating women (who require 5100 mg/day).
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Minerals — Potassium AI (mg/day) table

items.fact: RDA for calcium is 1300 mg/day for adolescents aged 9–18 years; decreases to 1000 mg/day for adults 19–50 years, then increases to 1200 mg/day for women ≥51 years and men ≥71 years.
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Minerals — Calcium RDA (mg/day) table

items.fact: RDA for iron is significantly higher in females of childbearing age (18 mg/day) compared to males of same age (8 mg/day), reflecting menstrual losses; pregnancy increases requirement to 27 mg/day.
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Minerals — Iron RDA (mg/day) table

items.fact: RDA for folate is 600 μg DFE/day during pregnancy to support neural tube development; lactation reduces requirement to 500 μg DFE/day.
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Vitamins — Folate RDA (μg/day) table, footnote b

items.fact: RDA for vitamin D is 600 IU/day (15 μg) for individuals aged 1–70 years; increases to 800 IU/day (20 μg) for adults >70 years, assuming minimal sun exposure.
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Vitamins — Vitamin D RDA (IU/day), footnote d

items.fact: RDA for vitamin A is expressed as retinol activity equivalents (RAE); values range from 400 μg RAE/day for infants to 900 μg RAE/day for adult males and 700 μg RAE/day for adult females.
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Vitamins — Vitamin A RDA (μg/day), footnote c

items.fact: AI for choline is 425 mg/day for adult women, 550 mg/day for adult men, and increases to 450–550 mg/day during pregnancy and lactation respectively.
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Vitamins — Choline AI (mg/day) table

items.fact: RDA for vitamin B6 increases with age: 1.0 mg/day for ages 9–13, 1.3 mg/day for adults 19–50, and 1.7 mg/day for adults ≥51 years.
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Vitamins — Vitamin B6 RDA (mg/day) table

items.fact: RDA for iodine is 150 μg/day for most adults, increasing to 220 μg/day during pregnancy and 290 μg/day during lactation to support fetal and infant thyroid function.
items.source_context: Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDA) and Adequate Intakes (AI) for Minerals — Iodine RDA (μg/day) table

items.fact: Tolerable Upper Intake Level (UL) for synthetic niacin is 35 mg/day for adults aged 19–70, applicable only to supplements and fortified foods—not naturally occurring food sources.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Vitamins table; footnote a: 'The UL for niacin and folate apply to synthetic forms obtained from supplements, fortified foods, or a combination of the two.'

items.fact: UL for vitamin B6 is 100 mg/day for adults 19–70 years; chronic intakes above this level may cause sensory neuropathy.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Vitamins table; adults 19–70 row shows Vitamin B6 UL = 100 mg/day.

items.fact: UL for synthetic folate is 1000 μg/day for adults; applies only to folic acid from supplements/fortified foods, not natural food folate.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Vitamins table; footnote a and adults 19–70 row: Folate UL = 1000 μg/day.

items.fact: UL for choline is 3500 mg/day for adults 19–70; excessive intake may cause hypotension, fishy body odor, or hepatotoxicity.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Vitamins table; adults 19–70 row shows Choline UL = 3500 mg/day.

items.fact: UL for vitamin C is 2000 mg/day for adults; higher intakes may cause gastrointestinal distress and increase risk of kidney stones in susceptible individuals.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Vitamins table; adults 19–70 row shows Vitamin C UL = 2000 mg/day.

items.fact: UL for preformed vitamin A is 3000 IU/day (≈900 μg RAE) for adults; does not include provitamin A carotenoids like beta-carotene.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Vitamins table; footnote b: 'The UL for vitamin A applies to the preformed vitamin only.' Adults 19–70 row: 3000 IU/day.

items.fact: UL for vitamin D is 4000 IU/day (100 μg) for all adults and most life stages; long-term intake above this may lead to hypercalcemia and soft-tissue calcification.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Vitamins table; adults 19–70 and all other adult/life-stage rows show Vitamin D UL = 4000 IU/day (100 µg).

items.fact: UL for supplemental α-tocopherol is 1000 mg/day for adults; applies to supplements, fortified foods, or combinations—not natural dietary sources.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Vitamins table; footnote c and adults 19–70 row: Vitamin E UL = 1000 mg/day.

items.fact: UL for sodium is 2300 mg/day for adults ≥19 years; consistent excess intake is associated with increased blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Minerals table; adults 19–50, 51–70, and >70 rows all list Sodium UL = 2300 mg/day.

items.fact: UL for calcium is 2500 mg/day for adults 19–50, decreasing to 2000 mg/day after age 51; high intakes may impair iron/zinc absorption and increase kidney stone risk.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Minerals table; Calcium UL = 2500 mg/day (19–50), 2000 mg/day (51–70 and >70).

items.fact: UL for magnesium applies only to synthetic forms (supplements/drugs), not food; UL is 350 mg/day for adults ≥9 years.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Minerals table; footnote d and all age groups ≥9 years show Magnesium UL = 350 mg/day.

items.fact: UL for iron is 45 mg/day for adults; higher doses may cause gastrointestinal toxicity and oxidative stress, especially in non-deficient individuals.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Minerals table; adults 19–50, 51–70, and >70 rows list Iron UL = 45 mg/day.

items.fact: UL for zinc is 40 mg/day for adults; chronic excess can impair copper absorption, cause immune dysfunction, and reduce HDL cholesterol.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Minerals table; adults 19–50, 51–70, and >70 rows list Zinc UL = 40 mg/day.

items.fact: UL for iodine is 1100 μg/day for adults; excessive intake may trigger thyroid dysfunction, including hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Minerals table; adults 19–50, 51–70, and >70 rows list Iodine UL = 1100 μg/day.

items.fact: UL for selenium is 400 μg/day for adults; chronic intake above UL is linked to selenosis, manifesting as hair loss, nail brittleness, and neurological abnormalities.
items.source_context: Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (UL) for Minerals table; adults 19–50, 51–70, and >70 rows list Selenium UL = 400 μg/day.

items.fact: Folate is a water-soluble B vitamin critical for DNA synthesis and red blood cell formation; deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and increases neural tube defect risk in pregnancy
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Folate  310

items.fact: Vitamin B12 works synergistically with folate in DNA synthesis and nerve function; deficiency leads to pernicious anemia and irreversible neurological damage
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Vitamin B12  315

items.fact: Choline is a conditionally essential nutrient involved in phospholipid synthesis, neurotransmission (acetylcholine), and methyl-group metabolism
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Choline  318

items.fact: Vitamin C functions as a cofactor in collagen synthesis, enhances nonheme iron absorption, and acts as a potent antioxidant
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — 10.3 Vitamin C  322

items.fact: Vitamin A (retinol) and beta-carotene support vision, immune function, and epithelial integrity; deficiency causes night blindness and xerophthalmia
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — 11.1 Vitamin A and Beta-Carotene  340

items.fact: Vitamin D regulates calcium and phosphorus absorption for bone mineralization; synthesized in skin via UVB exposure and obtained from fortified foods or supplements
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — 11.2 Vitamin D  347

items.fact: Vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol) is the major lipid-soluble antioxidant protecting cell membranes from oxidative damage
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — 11.3 Vitamin E  353

items.fact: Vitamin K is essential for post-translational gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X and bone proteins like osteocalcin
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — 11.4 Vitamin K  354

items.fact: Water maintains blood volume, regulates temperature, transports nutrients, and supports acid-base and electrolyte balance
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — 12.1 Water and the Body Fluids  368

items.fact: Sodium is the primary extracellular cation regulating fluid balance, nerve transmission, and muscle contraction; excess intake is linked to hypertension
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Sodium  379

items.fact: Potassium is the principal intracellular cation that counterbalances sodium, supports heart rhythm, and helps lower blood pressure
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Potassium  383

items.fact: Calcium is vital for bone and teeth structure, muscle contraction, nerve signaling, and blood clotting; inadequate intake contributes to osteoporosis
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Calcium  384

items.fact: Iron is central to hemoglobin and myoglobin for oxygen transport and storage; deficiency causes microcytic hypochromic anemia
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Iron  405

items.fact: Zinc is a cofactor for over 300 enzymes involved in DNA synthesis, immune function, wound healing, and taste perception
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Zinc  414

items.fact: Iodine is required for synthesis of thyroid hormones T3 and T4, which regulate basal metabolic rate and growth
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Iodine  418

items.fact: Selenium is incorporated into selenoproteins such as glutathione peroxidase, playing key roles in antioxidant defense and thyroid hormone metabolism
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Selenium  419

items.fact: Fluoride strengthens tooth enamel by forming fluorapatite and inhibits dental caries; optimal intake prevents tooth decay without causing fluorosis
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Fluoride  422

items.fact: Maternal folate, iron, iodine, and energy needs increase significantly during pregnancy to support fetal growth and prevent complications like NTDs and preterm birth
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Chapter 15 Nutrition during Pregnancy  479

items.fact: Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for first 6 months; iron-fortified formula is alternative; cow’s milk is inappropriate before age 1 due to renal solute load and low iron
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — Chapter 16 Nutrition during Infancy  505

items.fact: Adolescents have increased requirements for calcium, iron (especially females), zinc, and protein to support rapid growth, peak bone mass accrual, and sexual maturation
items.source_context: Table of Contents   xi — 16.3 Nutrition during Adolescence  536
